title: Expression profiling, phenomics, and metabolomics revealed biochemical pathways regulating silicon induced tolerance to metalloid (As, Sb, Ge) toxicity in mungbean (Vigna radiata L. Wilczek).
author_string: Thakral, Vandana, Sudhakaran, Sreeja, Mahakalkar, Badal, Kumar, Sudhir, Tripathi, Durgesh K et al.
abstract: Metalloid toxicity is one of the principal abiotic stresses faced by plants, leading to the uptake of harmful metalloids disrupting photosynthetic apparatus, interfere with metabolic pathways, and induce oxidative stress. The study evaluates the role of silicon (Si) in mitigating metalloid toxicity by assessing its effect on photosynthetic efficiency, antioxidant defense mechanisms; and overall physiological responses in mungbean (Vigna radiata (L.) Wilczek). Non-invasive leaf imaging showed higher Fv/Fm values in Si supplemented mungbean under metalloid stress, indicating Si mediated improved photosynthetic efficiency. Antioxidant enzymes are crucial for scavenging excess reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated during metalloid toxicity. Metalloid treatment in mungbean plants significantly affected antioxidant enzyme activity, with Si application further enhancing CAT and GR activity. Additionally, Si treatment ameliorated the negative effect of increased malondialdehyde (MDA) and electrolytic leakage associated with metalloid toxicity, indicating its protective role in maintaining cellular integrity. Arsenic (As) and Si co-deposition suggested the protective role of Si in mungbean by reducing As toxicity, particularly in stomatal guard cells. Metabolomics analysis identified significant alterations in key metabolic pathways including the phenylpropanoid, flavonoid, amino acid, and terpenoid biosynthesis, in response to metalloid stress. These findings collectively highlight the Si mediated biochemical, physiological, and metabolic adjustments in mungbean under metalloid toxicity, providing critical insights into the protective mechanisms involved.
